profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Investigating pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward different pedestrian facilities (signalized/unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, physical barriers, shelters, colored paving) to help traffic engineers and planners design better pedestrian facilities
profiles.method_summary: Mixed-methods study combining: (1) observational study using video cameras to record pedestrian movements and calculate crossing compliance rates, and (2) user survey questionnaires distributed to 711 daily and occasional users of the study corridor to assess pedestrian perceptions and preferences
datasets: Grand River Avenue corridor in East Lansing, MI (1-km divided boulevard from Abbott to Bogue Streets)
Video data from 8 video cameras capturing pedestrian movements during peak hours (noon-peak and PM-peak periods)
Survey responses from 711 pedestrians (254 daily users, 457 occasional users)
baselines: signalized_intersection_crosswalks
unsignalized_intersection_crosswalks
unsignalized_midblock_crosswalks
non-striped_midblock_crosswalks

main_claims.claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian facility (83% reported preference) with high crossing compliance (71.2%)
Crosswalk location relative to pedestrian origin/destination is the most influential decision factor (90% cited this as important)
Pedestrian signal presence significantly influences crossing decisions at specific locations (74% reported influence)
Physical barriers (vegetation and concrete walls) influence pedestrian crossing decisions significantly (65% reported influence)
Survey responses show good agreement with observed behavioral data, validating the survey methodology

limitations.text: Study conducted at a single location (Grand River Avenue, East Lansing) near a university campus, limiting generalizability to other contexts
Survey sample primarily comprised university affiliates (students, staff, faculty), which may not represent general pedestrian population demographics
Data collection excluded rainy days and special events (football games, holidays), potentially biasing results toward typical conditions
Crosswalk influence area methodology assumes uniform pedestrian attraction within defined boundaries, which may oversimplify actual pedestrian behavior

future_work: conduct_similar_studies_in_different_urban_contexts
examine_relationship_between_safety_and_pedestrian_acceptance
assess_value_of_pedestrian_warning_signs_through_crash_conflict_analysis
investigate_driver_and_pedestrian_perceptions_of_right_of_way_at_intersections
evaluate_leading_pedestrian_intervals_for_improving_safety_and_compliance
profiles.notes: Study site featured multiple pedestrian facility types: signalized intersections (Abbott, Collingwood, MAC Avenue, Division), unsignalized T-intersections (Charles, Orchard), marked midblock crosswalks with/without shelters, non-striped midblock crosswalks with colored brick pavement, physical barriers (vegetation, concrete walls), and pedestrian warning signs. Statistical tests showed age and gender differences were not significant at 95% confidence level except for distance factor response among elderly pedestrians.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China, specifically addressing the high rate of signal non-compliance (red light running) which differs significantly from pedestrian behavior in developed countries
profiles.method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model considers external factors including policeman presence, behavior of other pedestrians, and vehicle flow. Parameters are calibrated using questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) and videotape data extracted via image processing software to determine gap acceptance behavior. The safety criterion DT (time gap) is calculated based on pedestrian estimates of vehicle arrival time and crossing time.
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students and 300 pedestrians)
Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT)
metrics: Time interval distribution of pedestrian arrivals
baselines: CORSIM (found inapplicable for pedestrian modeling in China)
SIMTRAFFIC (found inapplicable for pedestrian modeling in China)
Existing models from developed countries (not suitable for Chinese traffic conditions)

main_claims.claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrians' red light running and mixed traffic characteristics specific to Chinese cities

After adjusting parameters based on analyzing deviations between initial simulation results and field data, the simulation results agree with field results better (red light jumper proportion: 0.82 mean vs 0.85 field; violation follower proportion: 0.19 mean vs 0.18 field)
The model may be applicable in the micro-simulation of traffic systems in other developing cities with similar traffic characteristics
Pedestrians can be classified into two types (law-obeying and opportunistic) with proportions that vary with external factors, providing a more realistic representation of Chinese pedestrian behavior

limitations.text: Current model only models pedestrians' decision making according to instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicles changing states (accelerating, decelerating)
Calculation method of DT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical
Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to initial simulation results deviating from field data

future_work: Study pedestrian behavior when oncoming vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate, etc.)
Improve DT calculation method for turning vehicles
Extend model applicability for simulating Chinese city traffic more practically
Calibrate parameters for different intersections and traffic conditions
profiles.notes: The paper addresses a unique problem specific to developing countries - high pedestrian signal non-compliance rates. The model introduces 'violation followers' (pedestrians who follow others to violate traffic signal within 2 seconds). Parameter r2 was adjusted from 0.69 to 0.35 based on analyzing deviation causes. The study was conducted in Xi'an, China, with data collection from November 2003 to April 2004.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency as new factors to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrian behavior intentions.
profiles.problem_type: Behavioral prediction study using psychological theory
profiles.domain: Transportation safety, pedestrian safety, traffic psychology
profiles.method_summary: Survey-based study using questionnaires with scenario-based items about pedestrian red-light violations at intersections. Data collected in Dalian, China with 260 valid responses. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) was used to test four models: basic TPB model and three extended models adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency.
method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) framework
datasets: 260 complete and valid survey responses from pedestrians in Dalian, China
tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intentions
baselines: Model 1: Basic TPB (instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control)
Model 2: TPB + descriptive norm
Model 3: TPB + descriptive norm + perceived risk
Model 4: TPB + descriptive norm + conformity tendency

main_claims.claim_text: Instrumental attitude (perceived benefits like saving time or convenience) is a significant positive predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models
A model containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency (Model 4) provides the best predictive utility with CFI=0.996 and CMIN/DF=1.235

When descriptive norm is added to the model, subjective norm becomes non-significant, indicating that pedestrians' observations of family/friends' actual behavior (descriptive) have greater influence than perceived approval (subjective)
Conformity tendency is a strong predictor of violating behavior intention, indicating that pedestrians are more likely to cross against traffic lights when they observe other pedestrians doing so

limitations.text: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors; actual behavior should be observed for validation
Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability may affect results
Perceived behavioral control was not significant, possibly because the scenario was not sufficiently risky or the behavior was not challenging enough for the sample
Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may fail to realize or underestimate the danger of violating crossing behavior

future_work: Increase the number of items in each construct to improve measurement reliability
Investigate perceived behavioral control effects under scenarios with different difficulty levels (e.g., wider roads, higher speed traffic)
Conduct observational studies to validate self-reported behavior
Explore interventions targeting attitude change, social norm correction, and safety awareness
profiles.notes: This study extends TPB by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency. The survey used a scenario where pedestrians cross against red light at an intersection. All respondents were physically able to cross, which may explain non-significance of perceived behavioral control. The sample was 57.3% female, with 49.2% aged 18-24 years, 58.5% with undergraduate education.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections, specifically developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man phase of traffic light signals. The research addresses the gap that while pedestrian behavior has received attention, there has been little methodological development of pedestrian waiting time models at signalized intersections.
profiles.problem_type: Statistical modeling of bounded waiting times
profiles.method_summary: The paper proposes a new statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections. Starting from a simplified scenario for a particular pedestrian under specific traffic conditions using bounded Pareto distribution, the model incorporates vehicle time headway via a generalized linear model. Statistical unconditioning is applied to integrate the waiting-time distribution over the distribution of vehicle headway, resulting in a mixture distribution with four components. The EM algorithm is used for parameter estimation. The model produces a U-shaped distribution capturing the interaction between pedestrians and vehicles.
method_family: Statistical mixture modeling, bounded distribution models, EM algorithm
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days)
tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections
Analyzing pedestrian crossing behavior by age and gender
baselines: Exponential distribution (commonly used in queuing theory)
Pareto distribution
Bounded Pareto distribution

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed statistical model produces a U-shaped distribution that characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior, showing that a large proportion of pedestrians cross immediately after arriving and a large proportion wait for the entire red-man phase
The U-shaped distribution can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios depending on pedestrian types and traffic conditions
Risk-taking pedestrians have significantly shorter intended waiting times than risk-averse pedestrians, with the model showing median relationships w(1)0.5 < w(2)0.5 < w(4)0.5 < C
Young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers (higher r1, lower r4) while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse (lower r1, higher r4), with average waiting times increasing with age

limitations.text: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only one variable (pedestrian waiting time) explicitly modeled

Most existing multivariate approaches assume normal or exponential distributions which are not suitable for the bounded waiting time problem, limiting ability to analyze multiple risk factors simultaneously

future_work: Develop a multivariate modeling approach, based on the proposed model, for pedestrians' waiting times at signalized intersections that can account for multiple risk factors simultaneously
profiles.notes: The model was empirically validated with field data from Kunming, China. The 3-component discretized model (12) was selected over the 4-component model based on BIC criterion (1162.9 vs 1174.1). Goodness-of-fit test passed at 5% significance level (chi-square = 3.51, critical value = 7.81). Key finding: about 13.8% of pedestrians intended to cross immediately, 50.6% waited for entire phase, 35.6% waited then crossed.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The research investigates factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, addressing the high rate of pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. Over 270,000 pedestrians die annually in traffic crashes worldwide, with pedestrian red-light running identified as a main cause of pedestrian crashes at intersection areas.
profiles.method_summary: A binomial logistic regression model was established using survey data collected from Hefei, China. The SP (stated preference) survey included 15 items collecting pedestrian socioeconomic characteristics, trip features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, the Score test was used to identify significant variables, followed by iterative logistic regression to determine the final model with four significant predictors.
datasets: Survey data from Hefei, China (631 valid samples)
Peak period调查 times: 7:30-8:30am, 11:30am-12:30pm, 6:00-7:00pm on weekdays and weekends
baselines: Reference categories in logistic regression: trip purpose (go to work/school), time period (dawn), attitude variables (baseline groups)

main_claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Pedestrians going to work or school have higher red-light running probability compared to strolling pedestrians (odds ratio 0.394 for stroll group)
Red-light running probability during daytime is highest compared to dawn, dusk, and night periods
Pedestrians who admit they will run red-lights when in hurry are significantly more likely to do so (odds ratio 0.299)
Pedestrians who believe road facility quality affects their crossing behavior are more likely to run red-lights at intersections lacking monitoring equipment

limitations.text: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences
Study focused only on signalized intersections, not including other crossing facilities like midblock crossings with/without buttons
Individual crossing behaviors studied while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons/group behavior
Limited time and labor resources constrained data collection scope

future_work: Collect data at multiple intersections in other Chinese cities to validate findings
Apply same methodology to identify contributing factors in different urban contexts
Compare results across countries to understand how cultural and driving factors affect red-light running behaviors
Study pedestrian red-light running at various crossing facility types beyond signalized intersections
Investigate impacts of pedestrian platoons on individual crossing behaviors
profiles.notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function using the four significant variables. The paper contributes by incorporating pedestrian attitude variables which were seldom considered in previous studies.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Identify personal and environmental factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized intersections in urban Hong Kong, where pedestrian crashes constitute 25% of crashes at signalized intersections and 62% of road fatalities involve pedestrians.
profiles.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during weekdays, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians across 652 signal cycles. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong
6 urban intersection sites with high pedestrian crash history (each >10 pedestrian crashes in 5 years)
baselines: Model 1: random parameter logit without interaction effects
Model 2: random parameter logit with interaction effects (preferred model based on AIC)

main_claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667, 64.3% probability of lower propensity)
Older pedestrians have higher propensity of red light running compared to adults (odds ratio = 1.208), while adolescents have lower propensity (odds ratio = 0.834)
Presence of a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (odds ratio = 0.579), and increase in number of pedestrians waiting also reduces propensity (elasticity = -0.942)
When other pedestrians violate the red light in the same cycle, individual red light running propensity increases significantly (odds ratio = 4.023), demonstrating social influence effects
Higher traffic volume and higher percentage of heavy vehicles reduce red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.518 and -0.484 respectively)

limitations.text: Study focuses on factors affecting red light running propensity but does not measure safety outcomes or associated crash risk
Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not assessed despite its correlation with crash risk and severity
Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and socioeconomic characteristics (education, income) are not available in the observation survey
Random parameter approach accounts for unobserved heterogeneity but cannot fully capture individual-specific factors like personality traits and risk attitudes

future_work: Explore effectiveness of pedestrian signal countdown devices in improving perception and combating red light running
Develop variable pedestrian signal time responsive to real-time pedestrian volume
Investigate interactions between vehicular speed, pedestrian characteristics, and red light running decisions using comprehensive traffic flow data
Conduct attitudinal surveys to gauge safety perception and anticipated crossing behavior
Examine effects of social norms using experimental or attitudinal models
profiles.notes: Study addresses gap in literature by examining both individual-level (pedestrian demographics) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors together; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence and behavior of other pedestrians; Hong Kong pedestrian signals have three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red - no countdown display exists; Model 2 with interaction effects (gender x traffic volume, gender x others violated, elderly x companion, elderly x others violated) provides superior fit (AIC: 2750.1 vs 2776.6); Random parameter for gender effect is statistically significant, justifying use of random parameter logit to account for unobserved heterogeneity

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions in India, including crossing speed, signal compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interaction
profiles.method_summary: Video-based field observation of 775 pedestrians at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours. Statistical analysis using ANOVA, Pearson correlation coefficient, and Student t tests to identify significant factors. Logistic regression models developed to predict pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction.
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
baselines: Indian_Road_Congress_standard_walking_speed_1.2_m/s

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with an observed average of 1.34 m/s
Gender, age, and departure signal phase significantly affect pedestrian crossing speed variations (ANOVA test, p<0.05)
Gender and group size are the most significant factors affecting pedestrian compliance behavior with traffic signals
Approaching vehicle type and suitable gap between pedestrian and vehicle significantly influence pedestrian-vehicular interactions in crosswalks
Male pedestrians have higher noncompliance rates (OR=0.569 for females vs males) and form a major portion of pedestrian-vehicular interactions compared to female pedestrians

limitations.text: Study limited to only 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, India - may not generalize to other cities or regions
Data collected only during morning and evening peak hours - does not capture off-peak behavior
Sample size of 775 pedestrians may not be sufficiently large for robust modeling of all behavioral factors

future_work: Collect larger sample sizes to improve statistical robustness
Analyze pedestrian arrival patterns with influencing parameters
Extend study to more intersections in different cities
Develop pedestrian delay models and pedestrian level of service models
Investigate pedestrian crossing behavior including arrival patterns
profiles.notes: Study introduces Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as a new metric; Logistic regression models developed for both noncompliance and interaction prediction; Design crossing speeds determined: 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians, 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians; Only 44% of pedestrians complied with traffic signals - high noncompliance in Mumbai; Mixed traffic conditions characterized by diverse vehicle types (car, auto/van, taxi, two-wheeler, HCV)

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to predict pedestrian crossing decisions at signalized intersections for connected vehicle applications, specifically to reduce collisions between pedestrians and turning vehicles at intersections
profiles.domain: Intelligent Transportation Systems, Autonomous Driving, Connected Vehicles
profiles.method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among traffic signal context, pedestrian decisions (cross/wait), motion types (standing/walking/running), and physical movement. Uses particle filter for Bayesian filtering to estimate pedestrian states including position, crossing decision, and motion type from traffic signal phases and pedestrian position observations.
method_family: Probabilistic Graphical Models (Dynamic Bayesian Networks) with Particle Filter Inference
datasets: 289 pedestrian sequences collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan. Contains pedestrian trajectories, motion types, and traffic signal phases. 251 cross-pedestrians and 38 wait-pedestrians. Used 4-fold cross-validation for training/testing.
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait)
metrics: Precision values for decision and motion
Recognition accuracy at different time-from-decision (TFD) moments
baselines: Iryo-Asano et al. (2015) model - logistic regression using pedestrian state at onset of PFG only (distance to crosswalk and traveling speed at onset of PFG)

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed DBN+PF system recognizes pedestrian crossing decision with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation and wait decision with 85% accuracy after 3-4 seconds across different noise levels
The model correctly recognizes cross decisions with 98% precision and wait decisions with 86-92% precision at low noise (rn=0.1m)

The DBN approach integrates contextual information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian behavior modeling, enabling earlier and more accurate intention recognition compared to methods using only pedestrian physical states
The system can estimate pedestrian position with reduced error compared to raw observations, achieving mean positioning error of 0.08m at low noise and 0.47m at high noise (rn=1.0m)

The model outperforms the baseline Iryo-Asano et al. method in recognizing waiting behaviors after 2.0 seconds of observation, due to continuous state estimation rather than single-point classification

limitations.text: The model assumes straight-line pedestrian trajectories and cannot handle curved trajectories well, causing positioning errors in turn-cross scenarios
Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking motion due to overlapping speed distributions, with only 35-56% recognition rate for running
Larger observation noises cause delayed reaction in recognizing running motion and earlier false detection of standing, degrading motion recognition performance
The assumption that pedestrians make decisions at the onset of PFG may not always hold; pedestrians may change decisions after onset based on traffic situations

The model only considers distance to crosswalk as explanatory variable; additional contextual factors like crosswalk length, vehicle presence, and other environmental factors are needed for more accurate modeling
Wait decision recognition takes longer (3-4s) than cross decision (2s) because pedestrians do not always decelerate immediately after deciding to wait

future_work: Incorporate additional contextual information such as existence and positions of turning vehicles to model how traffic situations affect pedestrian decisions
Learn turning motion as a spatial feature to achieve path prediction for curved trajectories
Add more explanatory variables to the model (crosswalk length, pedestrian count, etc.)
Improve running vs walking discrimination by integrating visual features from images
Address pedestrians who enter the scene after onset of PFG by considering time elapsed from PFG onset
Develop the system for practical autonomous driving applications
profiles.notes: Research focuses on left-hand traffic (Japan) and near-side pedestrians approaching left-turning vehicles. Uses Japanese pedestrian signal phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), and PR (pedestrian red). The model considers that during PG pedestrians always have cross intention, and decisions are made at PFG onset. Computational efficiency: 5.1s to process 1.0s data (13 frames) using 2000 particles.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Automating the generation of literature review segments from PDF files of research papers, addressing the challenge of time-consuming and error-prone manual literature reviews due to the exponential growth in scientific publications
profiles.domain: Natural Language Processing, Academic Text Summarization
profiles.method_summary: Three approaches are implemented and compared: (1) frequency-based extractive summarization using spaCy with word frequency calculation and sentence weight ranking, (2) transformer-based abstractive summarization using Simple T5 fine-tuned on SciTLDR, (3) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with SciTLDR as knowledge base
datasets: SciTLDR dataset (Hugging Face) - 5,400 TLDRs from over 3,200 scientific papers with source (abstract, introduction, conclusion) and target (summaries) fields
tasks: Automated literature review segment generation from PDF inputs
baselines: spaCy frequency-based approach
Simple T5 transformer model
GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 LLM with RAG

main_claims.claim_text: The LLM-based model (GPT-3.5-turbo) achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score of 0.364, outperforming T5 (0.268) and spaCy (0.257) in generating literature reviews
All three NLP approaches (spaCy, T5, and GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125) can produce satisfactory results in automating literature review generation
The paper proposes and implements three unique end-to-end pipelines allowing users to generate literature reviews directly from PDF and DOI inputs with a GUI tool
Large language models are superior to older NLP approaches (frequency-based and transformer-based) for literature review automation based on ROUGE-N scores

limitations.text: The models are not yet fully capable of automating literature reviews and require human reviewers (from related work by Yuan et al.)
The paper lacks practical validation across diverse domains
The system only generates literature review segments (summary of individual papers), not comprehensive literature review sections with synthesis across papers
ROUGE scores may not fully capture semantic quality and coherence of generated literature reviews

future_work: Add more GUI functionality including model options and output size controls
Explore additional models such as BERT, Gemini, and LLaMA to potentially achieve better results
Enhance the effectiveness and applicability of the developed system tool
profiles.notes: The SciTLDR dataset contains both author-written and expert-derived TLDRs of scientific documents; All three systems take PDF files or DOIs as input and produce summarized literature review segments; The final GUI tool was developed using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 as the backend; The ROUGE evaluation was performed on test data from the SciTLDR dataset; spaCy uses top 10% of sentences based on calculated sentence weights as final output

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in traffic system micro-simulation in China, specifically addressing the high rate of signal non-compliance (red light running) which differs significantly from pedestrian behavior in developed countries
matrix.method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model considers external factors including policeman presence, behavior of other pedestrians, and vehicle flow. Parameters are calibrated using questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) and videotape data extracted via image processing software to determine gap acceptance behavior. The safety criterion DT (time gap) is calculated based on pedestrian estimates of vehicle arrival time and crossing time.
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian red light running simulation; Gap acceptance decision modeling; Mixed traffic flow simulation
matrix.datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students and 300 pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT)
matrix.metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Time interval distribution of pedestrian arrivals
matrix.claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrians' red light running and mixed traffic characteristics specific to Chinese cities
matrix.limitations: Current model only models pedestrians' decision making according to instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicles changing states (accelerating, decelerating) | Calculation method of DT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to initial simulation results deviating from field data
matrix.notes: The paper addresses a unique problem specific to developing countries - high pedestrian signal non-compliance rates. The model introduces 'violation followers' (pedestrians who follow others to violate traffic signal within 2 seconds). Parameter r2 was adjusted from 0.69 to 0.35 based on analyzing deviation causes. The study was conducted in Xi'an, China, with data collection from November 2003 to April 2004.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency as new factors to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrian behavior intentions.
matrix.method_summary: Survey-based study using questionnaires with scenario-based items about pedestrian red-light violations at intersections. Data collected in Dalian, China with 260 valid responses. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) was used to test four models: basic TPB model and three extended models adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency.
matrix.method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) framework
matrix.tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intentions
matrix.datasets: 260 complete and valid survey responses from pedestrians in Dalian, China
matrix.metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI; Cronbach's alpha; Regression weights
matrix.claim_text: Instrumental attitude (perceived benefits like saving time or convenience) is a significant positive predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models
matrix.limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors; actual behavior should be observed for validation | Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain internal reliability may affect results | Perceived behavioral control was not significant, possibly because the scenario was not sufficiently risky or the behavior was not challenging enough for the sample | Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may fail to realize or underestimate the danger of violating crossing behavior
matrix.notes: This study extends TPB by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency. The survey used a scenario where pedestrians cross against red light at an intersection. All respondents were physically able to cross, which may explain non-significance of perceived behavioral control. The sample was 57.3% female, with 49.2% aged 18-24 years, 58.5% with undergraduate education.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections, specifically developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man phase of traffic light signals. The research addresses the gap that while pedestrian behavior has received attention, there has been little methodological development of pedestrian waiting time models at signalized intersections.
matrix.method_summary: The paper proposes a new statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections. Starting from a simplified scenario for a particular pedestrian under specific traffic conditions using bounded Pareto distribution, the model incorporates vehicle time headway via a generalized linear model. Statistical unconditioning is applied to integrate the waiting-time distribution over the distribution of vehicle headway, resulting in a mixture distribution with four components. The EM algorithm is used for parameter estimation. The model produces a U-shaped distribution capturing the interaction between pedestrians and vehicles.
matrix.method_family: Statistical mixture modeling, bounded distribution models, EM algorithm
matrix.tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections; Analyzing pedestrian crossing behavior by age and gender
matrix.datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days)
matrix.metrics: Goodness-of-fit test (chi-square); Parameter estimates with standard errors; Proportion estimates (r1, r2, r4); Median and average waiting times
matrix.claim_text: The proposed statistical model produces a U-shaped distribution that characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior, showing that a large proportion of pedestrians cross immediately after arriving and a large proportion wait for the entire red-man phase
matrix.limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only one variable (pedestrian waiting time) explicitly modeled | Most existing multivariate approaches assume normal or exponential distributions which are not suitable for the bounded waiting time problem, limiting ability to analyze multiple risk factors simultaneously
matrix.notes: The model was empirically validated with field data from Kunming, China. The 3-component discretized model (12) was selected over the 4-component model based on BIC criterion (1162.9 vs 1174.1). Goodness-of-fit test passed at 5% significance level (chi-square = 3.51, critical value = 7.81). Key finding: about 13.8% of pedestrians intended to cross immediately, 50.6% waited for entire phase, 35.6% waited then crossed.

matrix.claim_text: Young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers (higher r1, lower r4) while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse (lower r1, higher r4), with average waiting times increasing with age
matrix.limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only one variable (pedestrian waiting time) explicitly modeled | Most existing multivariate approaches assume normal or exponential distributions which are not suitable for the bounded waiting time problem, limiting ability to analyze multiple risk factors simultaneously
matrix.notes: The model was empirically validated with field data from Kunming, China. The 3-component discretized model (12) was selected over the 4-component model based on BIC criterion (1162.9 vs 1174.1). Goodness-of-fit test passed at 5% significance level (chi-square = 3.51, critical value = 7.81). Key finding: about 13.8% of pedestrians intended to cross immediately, 50.6% waited for entire phase, 35.6% waited then crossed.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: The research investigates factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, addressing the high rate of pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. Over 270,000 pedestrians die annually in traffic crashes worldwide, with pedestrian red-light running identified as a main cause of pedestrian crashes at intersection areas.
matrix.method_summary: A binomial logistic regression model was established using survey data collected from Hefei, China. The SP (stated preference) survey included 15 items collecting pedestrian socioeconomic characteristics, trip features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, the Score test was used to identify significant variables, followed by iterative logistic regression to determine the final model with four significant predictors.
matrix.tasks: identify_significant_factors_affecting_pedestrian_red_light_running; predict_probability_of_red_light_running_behavior; evaluate_impacts_of_trip_characteristics_and_attitudes
matrix.datasets: Survey data from Hefei, China (631 valid samples); Collected May-June 2015; Six typical signalized intersections selected; Peak period调查 times: 7:30-8:30am, 11:30am-12:30pm, 6:00-7:00pm on weekdays and weekends
matrix.metrics: Score test significance (95% confidence level); Odds ratios; Model coefficients; P-values
matrix.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
matrix.limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study focused only on signalized intersections, not including other crossing facilities like midblock crossings with/without buttons | Individual crossing behaviors studied while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons/group behavior | Limited time and labor resources constrained data collection scope
matrix.notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function using the four significant variables. The paper contributes by incorporating pedestrian attitude variables which were seldom considered in previous studies.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Identify personal and environmental factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized intersections in urban Hong Kong, where pedestrian crashes constitute 25% of crashes at signalized intersections and 62% of road fatalities involve pedestrians.
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during weekdays, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians across 652 signal cycles. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
matrix.tasks: analyze_pedestrian_red_light_running_behavior; identify_personal_factors_affecting_violation_propensity; identify_environmental_factors_affecting_violation_propensity; examine_interaction_effects_between_factors
matrix.datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; 6 urban intersection sites with high pedestrian crash history (each >10 pedestrian crashes in 5 years)
matrix.metrics: red_light_running_violation_binary_outcome; odds_ratios; marginal_effects; elasticities; AIC_for_model_comparison
matrix.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667, 64.3% probability of lower propensity)
matrix.limitations: Study focuses on factors affecting red light running propensity but does not measure safety outcomes or associated crash risk | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not assessed despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and socioeconomic characteristics (education, income) are not available in the observation survey | Random parameter approach accounts for unobserved heterogeneity but cannot fully capture individual-specific factors like personality traits and risk attitudes
matrix.notes: Study addresses gap in literature by examining both individual-level (pedestrian demographics) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors together; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence and behavior of other pedestrians; Hong Kong pedestrian signals have three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red - no countdown display exists; Model 2 with interaction effects (gender x traffic volume, gender x others violated, elderly x companion, elderly x others violated) provides superior fit (AIC: 2750.1 vs 2776.6); Random parameter for gender effect is statistically significant, justifying use of random parameter logit to account for unobserved heterogeneity

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions in India, including crossing speed, signal compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interaction
matrix.method_summary: Video-based field observation of 775 pedestrians at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours. Statistical analysis using ANOVA, Pearson correlation coefficient, and Student t tests to identify significant factors. Logistic regression models developed to predict pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction.
matrix.tasks: pedestrian_crossing_speed_analysis; pedestrian_compliance_behavior_analysis; pedestrian-vehicular_interaction_analysis
matrix.datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
matrix.metrics: crossing_speed_(m/s); compliance_rate_(%); CSDF_(Crossing_Speed_Deviation_Factor); odds_ratio_(OR)
matrix.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with an observed average of 1.34 m/s
matrix.limitations: Study limited to only 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, India - may not generalize to other cities or regions | Data collected only during morning and evening peak hours - does not capture off-peak behavior | Sample size of 775 pedestrians may not be sufficiently large for robust modeling of all behavioral factors
matrix.notes: Study introduces Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as a new metric; Logistic regression models developed for both noncompliance and interaction prediction; Design crossing speeds determined: 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians, 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians; Only 44% of pedestrians complied with traffic signals - high noncompliance in Mumbai; Mixed traffic conditions characterized by diverse vehicle types (car, auto/van, taxi, two-wheeler, HCV)

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to predict pedestrian crossing decisions at signalized intersections for connected vehicle applications, specifically to reduce collisions between pedestrians and turning vehicles at intersections
matrix.method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among traffic signal context, pedestrian decisions (cross/wait), motion types (standing/walking/running), and physical movement. Uses particle filter for Bayesian filtering to estimate pedestrian states including position, crossing decision, and motion type from traffic signal phases and pedestrian position observations.
matrix.method_family: Probabilistic Graphical Models (Dynamic Bayesian Networks) with Particle Filter Inference
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait); Motion type classification (standing, walking, running); Pedestrian position estimation
matrix.datasets: 289 pedestrian sequences collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan. Contains pedestrian trajectories, motion types, and traffic signal phases. 251 cross-pedestrians and 38 wait-pedestrians. Used 4-fold cross-validation for training/testing.
matrix.metrics: Confusion matrices for decision recognition; Confusion matrices for motion recognition; Precision values for decision and motion; Positioning error (mean and standard deviation); Recognition accuracy at different time-from-decision (TFD) moments
matrix.claim_text: The proposed DBN+PF system recognizes pedestrian crossing decision with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation and wait decision with 85% accuracy after 3-4 seconds across different noise levels

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Automating the generation of literature review segments from PDF files of research papers, addressing the challenge of time-consuming and error-prone manual literature reviews due to the exponential growth in scientific publications
matrix.method_summary: Three approaches are implemented and compared: (1) frequency-based extractive summarization using spaCy with word frequency calculation and sentence weight ranking, (2) transformer-based abstractive summarization using Simple T5 fine-tuned on SciTLDR, (3) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with SciTLDR as knowledge base
matrix.method_family: Extractive summarization; Abstractive summarization; Retrieval-augmented generation
matrix.tasks: Automated literature review segment generation from PDF inputs
matrix.datasets: SciTLDR dataset (Hugging Face) - 5,400 TLDRs from over 3,200 scientific papers with source (abstract, introduction, conclusion) and target (summaries) fields
matrix.metrics: ROUGE-1 (precision, recall, F1); ROUGE-2 (precision, recall, F1); ROUGE-L (longest common subsequence); ROUGE-Lsum (summary-level evaluation)
matrix.claim_text: The LLM-based model (GPT-3.5-turbo) achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score of 0.364, outperforming T5 (0.268) and spaCy (0.257) in generating literature reviews
matrix.limitations: The models are not yet fully capable of automating literature reviews and require human reviewers (from related work by Yuan et al.) | The paper lacks practical validation across diverse domains | The system only generates literature review segments (summary of individual papers), not comprehensive literature review sections with synthesis across papers | ROUGE scores may not fully capture semantic quality and coherence of generated literature reviews
matrix.notes: The SciTLDR dataset contains both author-written and expert-derived TLDRs of scientific documents; All three systems take PDF files or DOIs as input and produce summarized literature review segments; The final GUI tool was developed using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 as the backend; The ROUGE evaluation was performed on test data from the SciTLDR dataset; spaCy uses top 10% of sentences based on calculated sentence weights as final output

Pedestrian red light running simulation.claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrians' red light running and mixed traffic characteristics specific to Chinese cities

After adjusting parameters based on analyzing deviations between initial simulation results and field data, the simulation results agree with field results better (red light jumper proportion: 0.82 mean vs 0.85 field; violation follower proportion: 0.19 mean vs 0.18 field)
The model may be applicable in the micro-simulation of traffic systems in other developing cities with similar traffic characteristics
Pedestrians can be classified into two types (law-obeying and opportunistic) with proportions that vary with external factors, providing a more realistic representation of Chinese pedestrian behavior

Predicting pedestrian violating road-cro.claim_text: Instrumental attitude (perceived benefits like saving time or convenience) is a significant positive predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models
A model containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency (Model 4) provides the best predictive utility with CFI=0.996 and CMIN/DF=1.235

When descriptive norm is added to the model, subjective norm becomes non-significant, indicating that pedestrians' observations of family/friends' actual behavior (descriptive) have greater influence than perceived approval (subjective)
Conformity tendency is a strong predictor of violating behavior intention, indicating that pedestrians are more likely to cross against traffic lights when they observe other pedestrians doing so

Modeling pedestrian intended waiting tim.claim_text: The proposed statistical model produces a U-shaped distribution that characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior, showing that a large proportion of pedestrians cross immediately after arriving and a large proportion wait for the entire red-man phase
The U-shaped distribution can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios depending on pedestrian types and traffic conditions
Risk-taking pedestrians have significantly shorter intended waiting times than risk-averse pedestrians, with the model showing median relationships w(1)0.5 < w(2)0.5 < w(4)0.5 < C
Young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers (higher r1, lower r4) while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse (lower r1, higher r4), with average waiting times increasing with age

analyze_pedestrian_red_light_running_beh.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667, 64.3% probability of lower propensity)
Older pedestrians have higher propensity of red light running compared to adults (odds ratio = 1.208), while adolescents have lower propensity (odds ratio = 0.834)
Presence of a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (odds ratio = 0.579), and increase in number of pedestrians waiting also reduces propensity (elasticity = -0.942)
When other pedestrians violate the red light in the same cycle, individual red light running propensity increases significantly (odds ratio = 4.023), demonstrating social influence effects
Higher traffic volume and higher percentage of heavy vehicles reduce red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.518 and -0.484 respectively)

sections.title: Introduction and Research Context
sections.text: This review synthesizes evidence from 10 papers examining Human error analysis, safety risk assessment, system reliability evaluation, measure spatial crossing compliance rate, measure temporal crossing compliance rate, with findings organized around 44 discrete claims drawn from a structured comparison matrix. The reviewed evidence employs Safety analysis / Human reliability analysis / System safety engineering, mixed methods, Microscopic traffic simulation, agent-based modeling as primary analytical frameworks. A persistent challenge in this literature is the heterogeneity in how studies operationalize crossing behavior constructs, report metrics, and handle context specificity — factors that collectively limit direct cross-study comparison. This review structures its analysis around methodological families, task-level performance patterns, metric reporting practices, and verified gaps (2 identified), moving beyond siloed paper summaries toward a structured evidence synthesis. The review is organized as follows: Section 2 examines methodological approaches and their representational tradeoffs; Section 3 addresses task-level findings and performance patterns; Section 4 evaluates metric reporting and comparability; Section 5 identifies verified research gaps and implications; Section 6 provides a field-level synthesis and research priorities.

sections.title: Methodological Approaches
sections.text: The Safety analysis / Human reliability analysis / System safety engineering family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The mixed methods family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Microscopic traffic simulation, agent-based modeling family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) framework family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Statistical mixture modeling, bounded distribution models, EM algorithm family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 10 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Task Scope and System Capabilities
sections.text: The reviewed literature addresses a heterogeneous set of crossing-related tasks, including Human error analysis, safety risk assessment, system reliability evaluation, measure spatial crossing compliance rate, measure temporal crossing compliance rate, assess pedestrian perceptions toward facilities, identify factors influencing crossing choices. This task diversity reflects the variety of questions the field has sought to answer: from predicting individual crossing initiation decisions to evaluating system-level safety outcomes. Performance claims vary significantly across task categories. Task-level performance is most consistently reported for Human error analysis, safety risk assessment, system reliability evaluation, where 44 discrete evidence claims were extracted from the reviewed studies. Reported metrics include: Not clearly specified in available chunks, spatial crossing compliance rate SCCR; temporal crossing compliance rate TCCR; pedestrian crossing volume; user survey response rates, Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Time interval distribution of pedestrian arrivals, CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI; Cronbach's alpha; Regression weights. Metric reporting heterogeneity is substantial — some studies report normalized rates while others report absolute counts or time-based measures — making cross-study performance comparison difficult without explicit normalization. A cross-cutting observation is that task definitions themselves vary across studies: what constitutes a 'crossing violation' or an 'acceptable gap' is operationalized differently across contexts, contributing to the apparent heterogeneity in findings. Standardizing task and metric definitions would substantially improve comparability across studies.

sections.title: Claim Synthesis and Cross-Paper Comparability
sections.text: Reported metrics in the reviewed literature span multiple dimensions of crossing behavior and traffic system performance, including: Not clearly specified in available chunks, spatial crossing compliance rate SCCR; temporal crossing compliance rate TCCR; pedestrian crossing volume; user survey response rates, Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Time interval distribution of pedestrian arrivals, CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA; PCLOSE; CFI; Cronbach's alpha; Regression weights, Goodness-of-fit test (chi-square); Parameter estimates with standard errors; Proportion estimates (r1, r2, r4); Median and average waiting times. Performance reporting is most complete for Human error analysis, safety risk assessment, system reliability evaluation tasks. Metric reporting practices vary considerably across papers in two key respects. First, normalization approaches differ: some studies express outcomes as normalized rates per unit time or per pedestrian, while others report raw counts. Second, the temporal resolution of reported metrics ranges from instantaneous (e.g., crossing initiation delay at a single phase) to aggregate (e.g., violation frequency over a multi-hour observation window). These differences make direct cross-study comparison difficult without explicit standardization. Addressing this heterogeneity will require consensus on metric definitions and reporting standards within the pedestrian behavior modeling community. The absence of such standards currently forces reviewers to rely on narrative synthesis rather than formal meta-analysis, limiting the precision of conclusions that can be drawn.

sections.title: Research Gaps and Opportunities
sections.text: The comparative analysis of 10 papers reveals 2 persistent gaps that current approaches have not adequately resolved. These gaps are documented through verified evidence from the review matrix and are categorized by severity below. Gap [afa374f6] (medium severity): Current coverage appears to underrepresent Chinese-language or Chinese-context studies.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [10d9c631] (medium severity): There are unresolved claim differences across papers under the same task, suggesting a need for clearer comparison criteria.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. These gaps collectively indicate that the field lacks the empirical foundation needed to support confident generalization of crossing behavior models across contexts, populations, and technologies. Prioritizing the most severe gaps in future empirical work will be essential for advancing both scientific understanding and the practical applicability of crossing behavior models.

sections.title: Discussion: Synthesis and Future Directions
sections.text: The evidence across 10 reviewed papers converges on a field-level picture in which pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections is influenced by a complex interaction of individual characteristics, traffic flow properties, signal timing parameters, and environmental context. Methodological approaches to modeling this behavior range from structural models grounded in behavioral theory to data-driven approaches optimized for predictive accuracy. Three stable conclusions emerge from this review. First, waiting time is among the most consistent predictors of crossing initiation and violation behavior across reviewed studies. Second, methodological approaches exhibit a persistent tradeoff between interpretability and predictive performance. Third, metric reporting heterogeneity across studies substantially limits the precision of cross-study synthesis. Two persistent tensions run through this literature. The first concerns the cross-context validity of gap acceptance thresholds: values derived from studies in North American and European contexts may not transfer to other traffic environments with different vehicle-pedestrian interaction norms. The second tension concerns the behavioral effects of automated vehicles: whether pedestrians recalibrate their crossing heuristics with sustained AV exposure remains unresolved due to the absence of longitudinal field studies. Resolving these tensions will require longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts. Priority should be given to addressing Gap [0] concerning cross-cultural threshold transfer and Gap [1] concerning longitudinal behavioral adaptation in AV environments — gaps that carry direct implications for both road safety policy and the design of pedestrian-accepting automated vehicle systems.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.text: The evidence across 10 reviewed papers converges on a field-level picture in which pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections is influenced by a complex interaction of individual characteristics, traffic flow properties, signal timing parameters, and environmental context. Methodological approaches to modeling this behavior range from structural models grounded in behavioral theory to data-driven approaches optimized for predictive accuracy. Three stable conclusions emerge from this review. First, waiting time is among the most consistent predictors of crossing initiation and violation behavior across reviewed studies. Second, methodological approaches exhibit a persistent tradeoff between interpretability and predictive performance. Third, metric reporting heterogeneity across studies substantially limits the precision of cross-study synthesis. Two persistent tensions run through this literature. The first concerns the cross-context validity of gap acceptance thresholds: values derived from studies in North American and European contexts may not transfer to other traffic environments with different vehicle-pedestrian interaction norms. The second tension concerns the behavioral effects of automated vehicles: whether pedestrians recalibrate their crossing heuristics with sustained AV exposure remains unresolved due to the absence of longitudinal field studies. Resolving these tensions will require longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts. Priority should be given to addressing Gap [0] concerning cross-cultural threshold transfer and Gap [1] concerning longitudinal behavioral adaptation in AV environments — gaps that carry direct implications for both road safety policy and the design of pedestrian-accepting automated vehicle systems.
